beforeAfter.description: This is how a typical operation changes when recurring work is automated and protected by explicit approvals.
beforeAfter.note: Example visualization for orientation. The final process model is tailored to your operating setup.

steps.title: Intake
steps.before: Cases arrive unstructured via email, PDF and calls.
steps.after: Inputs are captured automatically and assigned reliably.

steps.title: Prioritization
steps.before: Urgency is detected manually and often too late.
steps.after: Priorities are marked by rules and routed immediately.

steps.title: Data transfer
steps.before: Business fields are copied manually multiple times.
steps.after: Relevant fields are extracted and prepared in a structured format.

steps.title: Completion
steps.before: Status and evidence are fragmented and hard to evaluate.
steps.after: Status, approvals and audit traces stay continuously visible.

impact.title: Save time where routine work still blocks teams today
impact.description: Choose a typical use case. The numbers are intentionally framed as discussion input and show where automation can free up capacity in practice.
impact.assumption: Note: These figures are example assumptions for an intro call. Actual time savings depend on volume, process quality, exceptions and the surrounding system landscape.

document-processing.title: Document processing
document-processing.description: Documents are classified, key fields are extracted and handoffs become cleaner.
document-processing.note: This example model assumes recurring, structured document-heavy operations.

steps.title: Intake
steps.before: Documents have to be opened, reviewed and sorted manually.
steps.after: Documents are pre-sorted automatically by type and business context.

steps.title: Capture
steps.before: Required fields are searched and transferred by hand.
steps.after: Relevant values are extracted and provided in a structured way.

steps.title: Review
steps.before: Gaps and mistakes often surface late in the process.
steps.after: Exceptions are flagged early and reviewed deliberately.

steps.title: Handover
steps.before: The next team or system receives incomplete context.
steps.after: Data and documents move forward in a structured handoff.

field-extraction.title: Extract specific values
field-extraction.summary: For example amounts, deadlines, contacts or file references are captured automatically.

inbox-routing.title: Route inbound documents
inbox-routing.summary: Documents are assigned directly to the right case, team or processing path.
outcomes: Less idle time in the inbox

inbox-routing.title: Inbound routing
inbox-routing.description: Incoming items are recognized, prioritized and handed over to the right role or system using explicit rules.
inbox-routing.note: Suitable for recurring routing rules and clear ownership models.

steps.title: Capture
steps.before: Cases arrive through shared inboxes and fragmented channels.
steps.after: All inputs are captured centrally and structured immediately.

steps.title: Prioritize
steps.before: Urgency and ownership depend on tacit individual knowledge.
steps.after: Rules mark urgency and identify the right target role automatically.

steps.title: Route
steps.before: Cases are forwarded multiple times or wait too long.
steps.after: The next handling step is triggered directly.

steps.title: Status
steps.before: Operational status is difficult to track transparently.
steps.after: Status and results stay visible along the flow.

role-assignment.title: Assign by role
role-assignment.summary: Cases are handed over directly to the right role, queue or responsible team.

priority-routing.title: Route by urgency
priority-routing.summary: Time-critical items are marked and escalated automatically.

approvals-handover.title: Approvals and handovers
approvals-handover.description: Control points, approvals and next steps are prepared in a structured way without removing accountability.
approvals-handover.note: Particularly useful where explicit approvals or controlled handoffs are part of the business process.

steps.title: Pre-check
steps.before: Approval readiness is prepared manually and unevenly documented.
steps.after: Cases are prepared with required information and plausibility checks.

steps.title: Approval
steps.before: Decisions run through ad-hoc mail and tacit knowledge.
steps.after: Approvals follow explicit roles and documented criteria.

steps.title: Handover
steps.before: Next actions start late or without enough context.
steps.after: Follow-up steps start directly with the required information.

steps.title: Evidence
steps.before: The decision chain is hard to reconstruct later.
steps.after: Approval and handover remain transparently documented.

approval-precheck.title: Prepare approvals
approval-precheck.summary: Required data, relevance and formal completeness are checked before the decision.

handover-checkpoints.title: Secure handovers
handover-checkpoints.summary: The next process step starts with clear documentation, rules and checkpoints.

knowledge-access.title: Knowledge access (RAG)
knowledge-access.description: Internal content is prepared so answers become faster, more contextual and easier to validate.
knowledge-access.note: Suitable for policy bases, internal documentation and recurring knowledge requests.

steps.title: Sources
steps.before: Knowledge is spread across PDFs, folders, wiki pages and email threads.
steps.after: Relevant sources are connected and structured centrally.

steps.title: Query
steps.before: Teams search manually or interrupt colleagues for context.
steps.after: Questions are matched against the right internal source context.

steps.title: Answer
steps.before: Answers are inconsistent and often hard to verify.
steps.after: Responses come with traceable source context.

steps.title: Next step
steps.before: Results still have to be collected manually afterward.
steps.after: Useful knowledge is ready for the next operational step.

internal-guidelines.title: Find internal guidelines
internal-guidelines.summary: Teams access policies, work instructions and standards faster.

case-context-search.title: Case-related research
case-context-search.summary: Relevant case context and internal knowledge are pulled together faster.

phone-prequalification.title: Phone prequalification
phone-prequalification.description: Recurring first-contact information is captured cleanly, categorized and prepared for the right team member.
phone-prequalification.note: Suitable for structured first contact, standard requests and defined handoff paths to humans.

steps.title: First contact
steps.before: Key information is captured inconsistently across calls.
steps.after: Relevant base information is captured in a structured way.

steps.title: Qualification
steps.before: The same routine questions are repeated manually.
steps.after: Requests are pre-qualified by type, urgency and ownership.

steps.title: Handover

steps.title: Follow-up
steps.before: Documentation and callback logic are handled afterward.
steps.after: Notes and next steps are already prepared.

callback-preparation.title: Prepare callbacks

aiPage.complianceTitle: EU AI Act — orientation (not legal advice)

timeline.text: The AI Act entered into force.
Prohibitions for certain AI practices started applying.
Additional key obligations start applying — including internal AI systems.

aiPage.complianceNote: Note: This is technical orientation and does not replace legal advice.
aiPage.riskTitle: Why local operation instead of cloud AI?
nonLocalAiRisks: Opaque data flows make auditability and control obligations harder to fulfill.
Processing outside your infrastructure increases privacy and confidentiality risks.
Limited traceability of model output weakens the quality of business decisions.
Generic cloud AI rarely adapts to internal requirements and constraints.
aiPage.riskNote: Local operation means: you retain control over data, models and decision logic.
aiPage.sectorNote: We design an operating model that aligns with privacy requirements, confidentiality obligations and internal approvals.
